title: Unsayable by Michael Cunningham review – The Hours author on the insecurities that powered his creative life
description:
The American novelist and screenwriter’s memoir covers sex work, Virginia Woolf and Hollywood success

As an eight-year-old in comfortable, solid, suburban California, Michael Cunningham felt compelled to make art before he quite understood what it was, or could do: a painting of souls ascending to heaven, for example, in which paradise resembled the Griffith Park Observatory and the dead were all depicted in profile because their creator felt unequal to the challenge of full faces. Ther
hook: Cunningham confesses the artistic hunger that drove him from sex work to screenwriting—ambition born from feeling like a ghost in suburbia.

title: Penelope Speaks: Rebecca Solnit on the Western Literary Tradition of Silencing Women
description: The following first appeared via Rebecca Solnit’s Meditations in an Emergency. Last weekend, the film The Odyssey was released and the Tate brothers were arrested in Florida at the behest of the British government, which seeks to extradite them to
hook: Solnit traces how Western literature has systematized women's silence—from Homer's Penelope to the present day, voicelessness as narrative tradition.

title: A Dying Art
hook: The last morticians reveal what death work teaches you about dignity, ritual, and how we've abandoned the people who touch our grief.

title: Motor City's director explains how he made the most unique revenge thriller of the year
description: The filmmaker discusses Alan Ritchson, visual storytelling, unforgettable needle drops, and why "the first person to speak usually loses."
hook: Motor City's director on visual storytelling so precise dialogue becomes optional—a revenge thriller that trusts cinema over exposition.

title: Beckett the Prophet
description: "We are all, it seems, still waiting for Godot."
hook: Beckett predicted our present moment: we're all still waiting, still talking past each other, still unable to leave.
